preamble: The voiced alveolar plosive /d/ can undergo a process of vocalization, particularly in intervocalic positions, where it transforms into a high front vowel [i]. This vocalized [i] can then combine with an adjacent vowel, such as a preceding /e/, to form the diphthong [ei]. This represents a significant lenition process where a plosive consonant becomes a vocalic element.

examples.note: In Southern Gallo-Romance, the intervocalic /d/ in Latin 'crḗd(ĕ)re' vocalized to [i], which then formed the diphthong [ei] with the preceding vowel.

sources: Southern Gallo-Romance: Occitan and Gascon | Oxford Research Encyclopedia of Linguistics.
